choices.explanation: The passage reverses this relationship — Confucian scholars argued against maritime trade, and the emperor agreed with their position. The opposition came from scholars, not the emperor.

choices.text: Between Ming Dynasty Chinese treasure fleet merchants and Portuguese caravel merchants who were simultaneously competing for monopolistic control over Indian Ocean spice trade routes
choices.explanation: The Portuguese did not reach the Indian Ocean until 1498 — 65 years after Zheng He's voyages ended. Portuguese competition cannot explain the 1433 decision.

choices.text: Between Confucian ideological preference for agrarian self-sufficiency and the commercial and diplomatic benefits of maritime engagement
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly presents the Confucian scholars' argument against maritime trade — Confucian ideology valued agriculture, hierarchy, and land-based order, making commercial maritime expansion ideologically suspect regardless of its practical benefits.

choices.text: Between Tibetan Buddhist monks and temple hierarchies who supported continued voyages and Confucian government officials who opposed them primarily on grounds of religious incompatibility with Buddhist maritime expansion
choices.explanation: The passage presents the opposition as ideological (Confucian) rather than religious — the conflict was over governance philosophy and resource allocation, not religious competition between Buddhism and Confucianism.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"When the Black Death reached the city of Florence in 1348, the banker and writer Giovanni Boccaccio observed: 'The plague arrived not only from person to person, but from the garments and any object that the sick had touched.' He recorded that the city tried isolation measures — but ultimately, 'there was no medicine for it, and no prayer or physical barrier could prevent it from spreading.' Within months, perhaps half of Florence's population was dead."
— Adapted from Boccaccio's Decameron, 1353

questions.question: According to the passage, boccaccio's account of the Black Death most directly supports which of the following conclusions about plague transmission in medieval Europe?

choices.text: The Black Death epidemic was geographically unique to Western European Latin Christian civilization and did not affect other regions or religious communities across Afro-Eurasia
choices.explanation: The Black Death originated in Central Asia and devastated regions from China to the Middle East to Europe — it was a pan-Eurasian catastrophe, not exclusively European.

choices.text: Medieval European urban governments were completely unprepared for epidemic disease outbreaks and undertook absolutely no preventive measures to control plague's spread through their populations
choices.explanation: The passage states the city 'tried isolation measures' — demonstrating that Florence did attempt preventive action, even if ultimately unsuccessful.

choices.text: Medieval people understood disease could spread through objects and physical contact, even without understanding the germ theory of disease
choices.explanation: Boccaccio correctly identified that plague could spread through garments and objects (fomites), not just person-to-person — demonstrating practical observational knowledge of transmission mechanisms even without the scientific framework to explain them.

choices.text: Religious intercessory prayer and penitential processions were the only available and medically effective treatments for bubonic plague infection in 14th-century medieval European cities
choices.explanation: Boccaccio states prayer could not prevent spread — the passage does not endorse prayer as effective treatment, but rather notes its failure alongside other measures.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"Ibn Battuta described the city of Timbuktu: 'The sultan [of Mali] is a lover of justice and a person of upright life. The inhabitants of Timbuktu are Muslims who follow Islamic law. They are gentle people... the most common occupation among them is trade... Scholars come there from various parts of the world to learn Islamic knowledge under the learned sheikhs. Books are brought from various countries... and are more profitable than any other merchandise.'"
— Adapted from Ibn Battuta's Rihla, c. 1355

questions.question: According to the passage, ibn Battuta's description of Timbuktu most directly challenges which of the following assumptions about sub-Saharan Africa in the medieval period?

choices.text: That gold bullion was the sole commodity exchanged along trans-Saharan trade routes and that sub-Saharan Africa produced no other commercially valuable goods for Saharan merchants to export
choices.explanation: The passage describes books as profitable commerce — while the broader record shows gold and salt as primary commodities, the passage focuses on intellectual commerce, not gold alone.

choices.text: That West African rulers of the Mali Empire were uniformly hostile toward visiting Islamic scholars and actively banned all Islamic religious practice and scholarship within their territorial domains
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly describes the sultan as a just Muslim ruler and Timbuktu as an Islamic city — the rulers were patrons of Islamic scholarship, not opponents.

choices.text: That sub-Saharan West African cities like Timbuktu were geographically inaccessible and too isolated for scholars and merchants from North Africa or the Middle East to successfully visit and trade
choices.explanation: The passage shows Ibn Battuta successfully visiting Timbuktu, and scholars coming 'from various parts of the world' — demonstrating that Timbuktu was accessible, not isolated.

choices.text: That sub-Saharan African societies lacked sophisticated urban centers, Islamic scholarship, or participation in long-distance literary commerce
choices.explanation: Ibn Battuta describes a prosperous Islamic scholarly city where books were commercially traded — directly contradicting any assumption that sub-Saharan Africa was isolated, illiterate, or without sophisticated urban and commercial culture.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"In considering the significance of the Indian Ocean trade system, historians have debated whether it represented a unified 'world system' or merely a collection of overlapping regional networks. Those who argue for unity point to the common monsoon calendar, shared commercial practices, and the circulation of coins, credit instruments, and merchants across the entire basin. Those who argue for regional fragmentation point to the different languages, religions, and political systems in each sub-region."
— Adapted from a historiographical debate in Indian Ocean studies

questions.question: According to the passage, the historians arguing for 'unity' in the Indian Ocean trade system would be best supported by which of the following types of evidence?

choices.text: A Chinese porcelain shard found at an East African archaeological site, demonstrating direct material connections across the full extent of the Indian Ocean basin
choices.explanation: Physical evidence of goods traveling the full length of the Indian Ocean — from China to East Africa — directly supports the argument for basin-wide connectivity, demonstrating that individual items (and thus commercial relationships) spanned what the fragmentation argument would treat as separate regional systems.

choices.text: A royal decree issued by the Zamorin of the Indian Malabar kingdom of Calicut formally prohibiting all commercial trade with Chinese merchants, thereby demonstrating deep regional political divisions within the basin
choices.explanation: A trade prohibition would support the fragmentation argument, not the unity argument — it demonstrates political barriers to connectivity rather than integration across the basin.

choices.text: A modern geographical climatology study demonstrating that Indian Ocean monsoon wind systems did not extend as far south as the East African Swahili coast, making direct maritime trade connections across the full basin physically impossible
choices.explanation: Monsoon winds do reach East Africa and enable trade with Arabia and India — historical evidence confirms this trade. A study showing they don't would contradict both historical evidence and the unity argument.

choices.text: A comprehensive demographic and linguistic survey documenting that each major sub-region's population spoke entirely distinct languages with no shared vocabulary, supporting the fragmentation rather than the unity argument
choices.explanation: Linguistic diversity supports the fragmentation argument, not the unity argument — the unity argument would be strengthened by evidence of shared commercial practices, not linguistic differences.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"Between 1200 and 1450, Islam spread dramatically across sub-Saharan Africa — not through Arab military conquest but through the activities of Muslim merchants and the prestigious commercial connections they offered to African rulers. In West Africa, rulers of Ghana, Mali, and Songhai converted to Islam while retaining traditional practices. In East Africa, Swahili city-states developed a distinct Afro-Islamic culture that blended African, Arab, and Persian elements."
— Adapted from a history of Islam in Africa

questions.question: According to the passage, the spread of Islam in sub-Saharan Africa described in the passage most directly supports which of the following conclusions?

choices.text: African rulers converted to Islam only under military threat, because they had no independent motivation to adopt the religion
choices.explanation: The passage describes commercial and prestige motivations for conversion — access to Islamic merchant networks provided clear economic and diplomatic benefits that gave rulers independent, non-coercive reasons to convert.

choices.text: Sub-Saharan Africa was isolated from Afro-Eurasian trade networks and received no Islamic influence before 1450
choices.explanation: The passage directly contradicts isolation — Islam was actively spreading in West and East Africa through commercial networks during precisely this period.

choices.text: Islam completely replaced indigenous African religious practices in all regions it reached
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly states West African rulers 'retained traditional practices' alongside Islam — syncretism, not complete replacement, characterized African Islam.

choices.text: Commercial contact was a more significant mechanism for Islamic expansion than military conquest in sub-Saharan Africa
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly states Islam spread 'not through Arab military conquest but through the activities of Muslim merchants' — commercial connection and the prestige of Islamic networks drove African rulers to adopt Islam, not armed compulsion.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The monsoon-driven Indian Ocean trade created a distinctive commercial calendar. Ships left East African ports in the winter months to reach India by spring; they traded through the summer and returned on the southwestern monsoon in autumn. This meant merchant communities often stayed in port cities for months, maintaining permanent diaspora communities with their own mosques, laws, and neighborhoods."
— Adapted from a study of Indian Ocean port cities

questions.question: According to the passage, the seasonal commercial pattern described in the passage most directly contributed to which of the following characteristics of Indian Ocean port cities?

choices.text: The development of luxury tourism as wealthy Europeans traveled to Indian Ocean ports during pleasant weather seasons
choices.explanation: Europeans had no significant presence in the Indian Ocean before the late 15th century — European tourism to Indian Ocean ports is an anachronistic concept for the 1200–1450 period.

choices.text: The monopolization of Indian Ocean trade by a single dominant civilization that controlled monsoon forecasting
choices.explanation: No civilization monopolized the Indian Ocean through weather knowledge — the predictable public nature of monsoon patterns meant all sailors could use them, enabling competition among Arab, Indian, Chinese, and Malay merchants.

choices.text: The development of cosmopolitan, multicultural port cities with permanent diaspora merchant communities from different regions
choices.explanation: Because monsoon cycles forced months-long stays, merchants established permanent communities in foreign ports — creating the multicultural character of cities like Malacca, Calicut, and Kilwa with coexisting merchant diasporas.

choices.text: The replacement of monsoon sailing with overland Silk Roads trade after merchants realized seasonal restrictions were too inconvenient
choices.explanation: Indian Ocean trade continued and expanded throughout 1200–1450 — seasonal restrictions were a manageable feature that merchants organized around, not a reason to abandon maritime trade for overland routes.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The Mongol Empire at its height stretched from the Pacific Ocean to the Danube River, but it never functioned as a unified state. By the time of Kublai Khan (r. 1260–1294), the four major khanates operated as separate entities with different religions, economies, and cultures. The Yuan Dynasty in China, the Il-Khanate in Persia, the Chagatai Khanate in Central Asia, and the Golden Horde in Russia were connected by dynastic ties but governed independently."
— Adapted from a political history of the Mongol Empire

questions.question: According to the passage, the political fragmentation of the Mongol Empire described in the passage most directly explains which of the following?

choices.text: Why the Mongols were never able to establish a functioning trade network because they remained politically fragmented from the start
choices.explanation: The Mongol trade network did function — the Pax Mongolica produced genuinely high trade volumes. The passage is about eventual fragmentation, not an inability to establish trade from the beginning.

choices.text: Why the Mongol Empire failed to conquer Japan and Southeast Asia due to internal political divisions
choices.explanation: The Mongol failures against Japan (1274, 1281) and Southeast Asia were primarily due to naval limitations and weather, not internal political divisions — these invasions occurred before the major fragmentation described in the passage.

choices.text: Why the Pax Mongolica's commercial benefits eventually declined as khanates began competing rather than cooperating
choices.explanation: When khanates operated as separate, competing entities, the unified legal framework and security that had enabled the Pax Mongolica broke down — merchants could no longer rely on consistent rules and protection across what had been a single imperial space.

choices.text: Why the Golden Horde converted to Buddhism after separating from the Yuan Dynasty
choices.explanation: The Golden Horde converted to Islam, not Buddhism — and the reason for conversion was contact with Muslim populations in their territory, not separation from the Yuan Dynasty.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"Between 1200 and 1450, several major historical processes operated simultaneously: Mongol conquests, the growth of Indian Ocean trade, the expansion of Islam, the spread of the Black Death, and the early stages of European maritime exploration. Historians debate whether these processes were independent or interconnected — whether one caused or enabled others."
— Adapted from a world history synthesis essay

questions.question: According to the passage, which of the following best exemplifies the interconnection between two or more of these historical processes?

choices.text: The Black Death pandemic was caused by early European maritime exploration that accidentally imported previously unknown epidemic diseases from the Americas into vulnerable Eurasian port populations
choices.explanation: The Black Death originated in Central Asian rodent populations and spread through Eurasian trade routes — European exploration of the Americas began only in 1492, decades after the worst plague years.

choices.text: Mongol political unification of Central Asia enabled the Pax Mongolica, which accelerated Silk Roads trade, which in turn facilitated the rapid spread of the Black Death to Europe
choices.explanation: This causal chain directly demonstrates interconnection: Mongol conquest → unified trade infrastructure → increased commercial traffic → faster disease transmission. Each step was causally connected to the next, demonstrating that these processes were mutually reinforcing rather than independent.

choices.text: The expansion of Islam across Afro-Eurasia in this period was driven exclusively by military jihad campaigns directly triggered by the Mongol army's catastrophic destruction of Baghdad and the Abbasid Caliphate in 1258
choices.explanation: Islam continued to spread primarily through commercial contact and Sufi missionaries, not military jihad triggered by Baghdad's fall — the passage describes multiple vectors of Islamic expansion operating independently of the Mongol destruction.

choices.text: The sustained growth of Indian Ocean maritime trade between 1200 and 1450 was entirely unconnected to Mongol activities because the Mongol Empire never established any political or military control over Indian Ocean sea lanes
choices.explanation: While the Mongols did not control the Indian Ocean, they disrupted overland routes, redirected goods toward maritime paths, and their conquests in China and Persia affected Indian Ocean trade — the networks were interconnected.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The ruins of Kilwa Kisiwani on the coast of present-day Tanzania include a 14th-century palace (Husuni Kubwa) that is the largest pre-colonial structure in sub-Saharan Africa, plus extensive mosque complexes and warehouse facilities. Chinese porcelain, Persian glass, and Indian textiles have been found in archaeological excavations alongside local goods. The site was declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1981."
— Adapted from a UNESCO archaeological report

questions.question: According to the passage, the archaeological evidence from Kilwa Kisiwani most directly demonstrates which of the following about East Africa in 1200–1450?

choices.text: The Indian Ocean trade was so destructive to local East African cultures that indigenous building traditions disappeared entirely
choices.explanation: The Husuni Kubwa palace demonstrates sophisticated indigenous East African architecture — trade enriched rather than destroyed local cultural production.

choices.text: East African societies were entirely dependent on imports from China and had no indigenous crafts or commercial production
choices.explanation: The archaeological report mentions 'local goods' alongside imports — East Africa exported gold, ivory, and other products rather than being purely dependent on imports.

choices.text: East African city-states were sophisticated commercial centers with connections to civilizations across the Indian Ocean world
choices.explanation: The presence of Chinese, Persian, and Indian goods at Kilwa proves commercial connections extending across the Indian Ocean — the monumental architecture demonstrates the commercial wealth those connections generated.

choices.text: Kilwa was a Chinese colonial outpost established by Zheng He to facilitate tribute trade with Africa
choices.explanation: Kilwa was an independent Swahili city-state predating Zheng He's voyages — it was not a Chinese colony but an African commercial city that traded with multiple Indian Ocean partners.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The Mongol conquest of China created the Yuan Dynasty, which governed one of the world's most commercially advanced economies. Kublai Khan's court attracted Persian administrators, Tibetan monks, Nestorian Christians, and Italian merchants — creating a cosmopolitan government that supervised paper money circulation, maintained the Grand Canal for grain transport, and encouraged trans-Eurasian trade. The Mongols had learned that a rich China paid more taxes than a destroyed one."
— Adapted from a history of the Yuan Dynasty

questions.question: According to the passage, which of the following best explains why the Mongols chose to maintain and develop Chinese commercial infrastructure rather than simply extracting wealth through plunder?

choices.text: The Mongols were compelled by military necessity to preserve Chinese commercial infrastructure because residual Chinese Song Dynasty military forces effectively prevented them from destroying the Grand Canal and banking networks
choices.explanation: The Mongols had already conquered China militarily — there was no Chinese military preventing them from destroying infrastructure. The choice to preserve it was strategic, not forced.

choices.text: The Yuan Dynasty Mongols lacked the administrative capacity to comprehend complex Chinese paper currency and credit systems and therefore left all existing Chinese commercial institutions entirely unchanged due to collective ignorance
choices.explanation: The passage describes Mongols actively managing paper money circulation and maintaining the Grand Canal — they were actively engaged with Chinese commercial systems, not passively leaving them unchanged out of ignorance.

choices.text: Kublai Khan converted to Tibetan Buddhism under the monk Phags-pa before conquering the Song Dynasty and was therefore personally committed to preserving Chinese Buddhist commercial and monastic institutions
choices.explanation: While Kublai Khan was influenced by Buddhism, his preservation of Chinese commercial institutions was driven by fiscal pragmatism — the passage specifically attributes it to tax revenue calculations, not Buddhist ethics.

choices.text: Sustained taxation of a productive economy yielded more long-term revenue than one-time plunder, giving the Mongols an economic incentive to preserve Chinese commercial systems
choices.explanation: The passage directly states the Mongols 'learned that a rich China paid more taxes than a destroyed one' — demonstrating that rational fiscal calculation, not cultural respect, led conquerors to maintain the productive institutions of the conquered.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"Among the many goods that moved along the Silk Roads and Indian Ocean routes, ideas and technologies were among the most significant. Mathematical zero, developed in India, reached the Islamic world by the 8th century and Europe by the 12th century via Arabic translations. Islamic astronomy, itself built on Greek and Indian foundations, influenced Chinese and European astronomical methods. Paper, printing, and the compass all moved from East to West; crops like cotton and sugar moved in complex patterns across multiple regions."
— Adapted from a history of knowledge diffusion

questions.question: According to the passage, the pattern of knowledge diffusion described in the passage most directly supports which of the following conclusions?

choices.text: All significant knowledge diffusion during this period moved exclusively in an eastward-to-westward direction along Silk Roads networks, with no meaningful intellectual or technological transmission flowing from Europe or the Mediterranean northward or eastward to Asian civilizations
choices.explanation: The passage describes complex multi-directional patterns — Greek philosophical and astronomical knowledge flowed eastward into Islamic scholarship, and sugar and cotton moved in 'complex patterns across multiple regions,' not exclusively east-to-west.

choices.text: The Islamic world served as a crucial intermediary in the diffusion of knowledge between Asia and Europe, synthesizing inputs from multiple civilizations and transmitting them to new recipients
choices.explanation: The passage shows Islamic scholars receiving mathematical zero from India and transmitting it to Europe; building Islamic astronomy on Greek and Indian foundations; and acting as intermediaries in multiple knowledge chains — demonstrating the Islamic world's central role in Afro-Eurasian knowledge diffusion.

choices.text: European civilization independently developed all the technologies listed and did not require external transmission
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly traces diffusion paths — mathematical zero came to Europe via Islamic translations of Indian mathematics, directly contradicting independent European development.

choices.text: All significant intellectual innovations originated in China and diffused westward through Islamic intermediaries to Europe
choices.explanation: The passage shows innovations from multiple origins — India (zero), China (paper, printing, compass), Greece (astronomical foundations) — not exclusively Chinese origins.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The Mongols did not build roads from scratch — they maintained and improved existing ones, inheriting the postal system infrastructure of the Tang Dynasty and the commercial routes of the Islamic world. What was new was that a single political authority now controlled both ends of the Silk Roads simultaneously, eliminating the diplomatic barriers that had complicated overland trade under rival empires."
— Adapted from a history of Mongol infrastructure

questions.question: Based on the passage, which of the following was the most significant Mongol contribution to Eurasian trade?

choices.text: The invention of paper currency in China, adopted and distributed across Central Asian markets by Mongol administrators
choices.explanation: Paper money was a Chinese innovation predating the Mongol Empire — the Mongols may have expanded its use, but its invention was not their contribution to trade.

choices.text: Political unification of territories at both ends of the Silk Roads that eliminated diplomatic barriers to overland commerce
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly identifies political unification as the new Mongol contribution — roads and postal systems already existed, but having a single authority control both ends eliminated the diplomatic fragmentation that had previously complicated trade.

choices.text: The imposition of Islam on Central Asian rulers and merchants, which unified commercial practices under a shared legal
choices.explanation: The Mongols were not all Muslims and did not require conversion of Central Asian rulers — their contribution was political unification, not religious homogenization.

choices.text: The Mongol construction of a continent-spanning network of paved roads and relay stations that shortened travel time
choices.explanation: The passage states the Mongols 'did not build roads from scratch' but 'maintained and improved existing ones' — the contribution was political, not technological innovation in road construction.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The environmental consequences of the Black Death were paradoxically positive for surviving populations in some respects. Abandoned farmlands in England returned to forest; with fewer mouths to feed, nutritional standards temporarily improved; and with labor scarce, wages rose for artisans and agricultural workers. Some historians argue the Black Death accelerated European economic development by forcing investment in labor-saving technologies."
— Adapted from a historical reassessment of the Black Death's long-term effects

questions.question: According to the passage, the historian's argument about the Black Death's long-term effects most directly illustrates which of the following?

choices.text: The recovery of abandoned European farmland into forest proves that human civilizations are inherently destructive to ecosystems
choices.explanation: The passage describes farmland returning to forest as a consequence of demographic collapse, not as a universal principle about human civilization and ecology.

choices.text: Disease epidemics uniformly produce positive economic outcomes by eliminating surplus labor and forcing surviving workers to demand
choices.explanation: The passage describes context-specific outcomes in Europe — it does not claim that disease universally produces positive economic outcomes regardless of context.

choices.text: The Black Death should be judged a net benefit to European civilization because the labor scarcity it created accelerated economic
choices.explanation: The passage does not make this moral calculation — it argues for complexity, not that development outweighed catastrophic death. Acknowledging positive long-term consequences does not require minimizing the deaths.

choices.text: Historical events can have both devastating short-term consequences and complex long-term consequences that are not entirely negative
choices.explanation: The passage acknowledges catastrophic death tolls while arguing that the long-term economic and environmental consequences were complex and sometimes positive for survivors — demonstrating that historical evaluation requires examining multiple timescales and perspectives.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"A Venetian merchant in Alexandria in 1350 wrote to his business partner: 'The plague has disrupted all commerce here. Half the merchants I knew are dead; the prices of spices have tripled because the ships from India are not arriving; my Egyptian correspondents have lost their families and cannot conduct business; and I myself dare not travel home because the roads are full of the dying.' His letter reached Venice three months later — too late to prevent his family from also contracting the disease."
— Adapted from a collection of medieval merchant letters

questions.question: According to the passage, the merchant's letter most directly illustrates which of the following about the commercial consequences of the Black Death?

choices.text: The Black Death remained confined to Mediterranean port cities such as Alexandria and Venice and had no measurable impact on merchants or goods traveling along
choices.explanation: The passage describes Indian ships not arriving in Alexandria — demonstrating that the plague's economic effects extended to Indian Ocean supply chains, not just the Mediterranean.

choices.text: Medieval merchants across Afro-Eurasia had no awareness of how epidemic disease spread between trading partners
choices.explanation: The passage shows the merchant was aware of disease risk — he 'dared not travel home because the roads are full of the dying,' demonstrating awareness that travel transmitted disease.

choices.text: Venice's geographic separation from Egypt insulated Venetian merchants from plague-related disruptions
choices.explanation: The passage describes a Venetian merchant in Alexandria conducting business with Egyptian correspondents — Venice was deeply connected to Egyptian trade, and the plague's effects in Egypt directly harmed Venetian commerce.

choices.text: The interconnected nature of Afro-Eurasian trade networks meant that plague's disruption of one commercial hub rapidly affected markets across multiple continents
choices.explanation: The plague in Egypt disrupted Indian Ocean supply chains (ships from India not arriving), inflated spice prices in Alexandria, and affected Venetian merchants' correspondence — demonstrating how commercial connectivity transmitted economic disruption across the entire system.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"By 1400, the cities of Kashgar and Samarkand in Central Asia had been transformed from small oasis towns into cosmopolitan commercial centers with populations of hundreds of thousands. Their marketplaces offered Chinese silk and porcelain, Persian carpets and metalwork, Indian cotton textiles, and Roman-style glass from the Mediterranean. Scholars and merchants from a dozen languages shared the same bazaars."
— Adapted from an archaeological survey of the Silk Roads

questions.question: According to the passage, the transformation of Kashgar and Samarkand into cosmopolitan commercial centers, as described in the passage, most directly resulted from which of the following?

choices.text: The Mongol Empire deliberately forcibly relocated entire populations from conquered regions in Persia and China to Kashgar and Samarkand
choices.explanation: While the Mongols did some forced relocation of skilled artisans, the cosmopolitan character of Silk Roads cities developed gradually over centuries through voluntary merchant settlement, not mass forced relocation.

choices.text: Chinese imperial administrators governed Central Asian oasis cities directly and required merchants from all regions to register and concentrate their trade
choices.explanation: Central Asian oasis cities were governed by local rulers and later Mongol khanates, not by direct Chinese imperial administration — their commercial development was organic, not Chinese-directed.

choices.text: Their strategic locations along the Silk Roads made them focal points for exchange among merchants traveling between China, India, Persia, and the Mediterranean
choices.explanation: Kashgar and Samarkand were intermediate oasis cities on the Silk Roads — their location on major overland routes made them natural accumulation points for goods, merchants, and cultures from all directions they connected.

choices.text: Islamic pilgrimage routes to Mecca passed through Kashgar and Samarkand, generating substantial religious tourism economies that sustained the cities'
choices.explanation: While some pilgrims traveled through Central Asia, Kashgar and Samarkand's commercial growth was driven by trade, not religious pilgrimage — the pilgrimage to Mecca did not primarily route through these cities.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
"The period 1200–1450 saw the peak and decline of several major exchange networks. The Silk Roads reached their maximum commercial activity during the Pax Mongolica, then declined after Mongol fragmentation and the Black Death. The Indian Ocean trade continued to grow. Trans-Saharan trade shifted from earlier gold-dominated routes to more diversified commodity networks. The relative fortunes of these networks shaped which regions would emerge as commercial powers in the next century."
— Adapted from a world history synthesis of the pre-modern trade era

questions.question: Based on the passage, which of the following best explains why the Indian Ocean trade was better positioned to continue growing after 1350 compared to the Silk Roads?

choices.text: Indian Ocean trade relied on geographic and climatological features (monsoon winds, ocean geography) rather than political stability, making it less vulnerable to political disruptions like Mongol fragmentation
choices.explanation: The Silk Roads required a unified overland political authority for maximum efficiency — Mongol fragmentation directly disrupted them. The Indian Ocean relied on predictable natural forces (monsoons) that could not fragment, making it structurally more resilient to political change.

choices.text: The Black Death never reached Indian Ocean port cities such as Calicut and Hormuz, leaving their commercial infrastructure undamaged while plague devastated Silk Roads hubs from Tabriz to Samarkand
choices.explanation: The Black Death did affect Indian Ocean ports — plague spread through maritime routes as well as overland ones, and Egyptian and Middle Eastern ports were devastated. This is not an accurate distinction between the two networks.

choices.text: China's Ming Dynasty actively subsidized Indian Ocean commerce after 1368 by providing merchant vessels to all traders willing to pay tribute at Nanjing, sustaining Indian Ocean networks through state patronage
choices.explanation: The Ming Dynasty discontinued maritime expeditions after 1433 and did not subsidize private trade — Indian Ocean growth was driven by private merchants and Southeast Asian/Arab commercial networks, not Ming subsidies.

choices.text: European merchants who had been excluded from overland Silk Roads trade by Mongol successor states' prohibitive taxation invested heavily in Indian Ocean shipping ventures after 1350
choices.explanation: European maritime expansion into the Indian Ocean began only at the end of the 15th century — European investment did not explain Indian Ocean growth in 1350–1450.
